takeaway: Total weekly revenue trends upward over the two years (~7% YoY growth) with visible recurring spikes. Personal Care shows the sharpest seasonal peaks (festival/summer), while Oral Care is the steadiest base-load category.

West is the largest market and East the smallest, a ~1.4x gap. Category mix is broadly consistent across regions, so regional differences are scale-driven rather than mix-driven -- relevant for how promo budgets should be weighted.

Personal Care peaks around Nov (festival gifting) and again in summer, swinging ~39 index points. Home Care peaks pre-Diwali (Oct); Oral Care is nearly flat, confirming staple behaviour. Any forecast must carry category-specific seasonality.

About 4% of SKU-weeks run a promo; depth clusters between 18% and 35%, with a meaningful tail of deep (>=30%) cuts. Those deep cuts are exactly the ones to scrutinise for profitability -- deep discounts on thin-margin SKUs are the classic value-destroyers.

Lower prices clearly coincide with higher volumes, and the premium/elastic SKUs show a steeper, wider price-volume cloud than the staple. Promo weeks (red) sit at the low-price/high-volume corner -- but because price moves for non-promo reasons too, elasticity is identifiable separately from the promo effect (formalised in the elasticity model).

Promo windows produce sharp, unmistakable volume spikes (here a naive promo-vs-base lift of ~154% for PC_SHAMP). The spikes return to baseline afterward with no obvious pantry-loading dip, which keeps the uplift estimation tractable.
